Cite typical values rather than vague descriptions. State assumptions explicitly when the question is ambiguous. Tone is direct and technical. Short over long.
What is the exact PWM signal a hobby servo expects?

50 Hz period (20 ms). Active-high pulse width 1.0 ms = full counter-clockwise (0 deg), 1.5 ms = center (90 deg), 2.0 ms = full clockwise (180 deg). Some servos accept 0.5-2.5 ms for extended range; check the spec. The duty cycle is a side effect (5-10 percent), not the control variable. Do not drive a servo from MCU 3.3 V power: it draws 100-500 mA peak which causes brownouts. Power the servo from a 5-6 V supply directly, share GND with the MCU, and the signal pin is fine at 3.3 V.

Set the current limit on an A4988 stepper driver.

Vref formula: I_max = Vref * 2.5 (for the original A4988 with R_sense = 0.05 ohm). To set 1.0 A target current, Vref = 0.4 V. Measure Vref between the small pot and GND while the motor is energized but stationary.

Procedure:
1. Power the driver, leave the MCU stepping pin idle.
2. Touch a multimeter probe to the metal pot screw and the other to GND.
3. Turn the pot until you read the desired Vref.
4. Increase the current by 10 percent if the motor stalls under load; decrease if the driver gets too hot.

R_sense values vary by clone (0.05, 0.1, 0.2 ohm); check your specific board's silkscreen. For DRV8825 the formula is I_max = Vref * 2 (R_sense = 0.1 ohm).

Drive a BLDC with an ESC. What is the PWM signal?

ESCs accept the same servo-style PWM: 50 Hz, 1.0-2.0 ms pulse. 1.0 ms = stopped, 2.0 ms = full throttle. Bidirectional ESCs use 1.5 ms = stop, 1.0 = full reverse, 2.0 = full forward. Arming sequence (most ESCs): power the ESC with the throttle signal at 1.0 ms for 2 seconds, then it beeps and is armed. Many ESCs also support OneShot125 (125-250 us) and DShot (digital) for FPV use. For hobby cars/boats just stick with 50 Hz servo PWM. Power: 5 V BEC (battery eliminator circuit) on the ESC can power your MCU; cap with 470 uF + 100 nF at the BEC output.

5 V relay module: how do I drive it from a 3.3 V GPIO?

Most cheap blue relay modules have a 5 V coil and an on-board NPN driver. The 'IN' pin draws ~5 mA via a base resistor to the NPN. From a 3.3 V GPIO this works only if the on-board NPN is wired with the input pulled to GND through the LED+resistor (active LOW), which most are: GPIO HIGH = relay off, GPIO LOW = relay on. Verify by measuring V_IN with the GPIO HIGH; if it's ~5 V, the input is open-collector internally and your GPIO is fighting the on-board pull-up; add a level shifter or use the active-LOW logic. Never connect the 5 V coil supply backwards; the flyback diode blows up.

WS2812B power budget for a strip of 144 LEDs.

Worst case (every pixel white at full brightness): 60 mA per pixel. 144 * 0.060 = 8.64 A at 5 V = 43 W. Realistic mid-bright color: 20 mA per pixel = 2.9 A. Power supply: pick at least 5 A of 5 V for moderate brightness, 10 A if you ever go full white. Inject power every 30-50 pixels: a single 5 V feed at one end of a long strip drops voltage along the strip and the far pixels turn yellowish (red shifts in over green). Use 18 AWG or thicker for the power injection wires.

Vibration motor (coin cell type): wiring and code.

ERM coin vibration motor draws 60-100 mA at 3 V, much more during start. Drive low-side: AO3400 MOSFET, 100 ohm gate, 100 k gate pull-down, motor between drain and the 3 V rail (or through a 47 ohm series if you want gentler vibration), 1N4148 flyback. Code: `analogWrite(GATE_PIN, dutyByte)` for variable intensity (PWM 100 Hz is fine for ERM). LRA (linear resonant actuators) need a real driver IC like the DRV2605L which generates the resonant AC waveform; do not PWM an LRA off DC, you'll just heat it.

How do I PWM a resistive heater for a small temperature controller?

Heater = pure resistor, no flyback needed. PWM frequency 1 Hz (slow PWM) works fine; you don't need fast switching for a thermal load. Use a logic-level MOSFET (IRLZ44N) low-side for a 12 V heater. The MCU loop computes duty from a PID on temperature error and writes the slow-PWM by toggling the gate every cycle. For better resolution, use a 30-second window with 100 buckets (300 ms each) to get 1 percent duty resolution. Always have a thermal cutoff: a self-resetting bimetal switch (60 C, 80 C, 105 C) in series with the heater is the safest backstop.

Buzzer: active vs passive, and how to drive each.

Active buzzer (built-in oscillator): apply DC 3-5 V, it makes its fixed frequency. Drive directly from a GPIO if current is under 20 mA (most), or via a 2N7000 if more. Passive buzzer (piezo element only): you supply the AC waveform, frequency = pitch. Drive with `tone(pin, hz)` on Arduino, or the LEDC peripheral on ESP32. Passive lets you play scales / melodies; active just goes 'beep'. For piezo, 2-4 kHz is loudest; below 1 kHz is quiet. 100 ohm series resistor between GPIO and piezo limits current.
